unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 4! In this unit you will explore the places in your community and the people who help you there. You will learn to name places like the hospital, the market, the court, the college and the garden, and to talk about helpers like the doctor, the officer and the sailor. You will describe places using words like quiet and busy, give clear instructions, ask questions, and explain your ideas with the word “because”. By the end, you will be ready to write your own report about a place in your community — just like Amal does in this unit's story.
unit.learningPath:
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a few community words each day.
Work through the seven short lessons in the Grammar file.
Read the Story file, “From Our Village to the County”, and answer the questions.
Work through the four skills below: Reading & Listening → Speaking → Writing → Activities.
Finish with the Self-Assessment to see what you have learned.

visual.alt: Children exploring places and services in their community

rubrics.level4: Uses target words accurately and naturally

rubrics.level1: Capital letters and punctuation need support

outcomes.learningOutcome: Name places in your community and the people who work there.
Describe a place using adjectives and opposites, like quiet and busy.
Give simple instructions using imperatives (“Exit the court quietly.”).
Talk about routines using the simple present (“The doctor works at the hospital.”).
Ask questions with do and does (“Where does the college open?”).
Say what you can see, using can see (“I can see the market.”).
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence
Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

outcomes.learningOutcome: Use some and a lot of to talk about amounts.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

readings.title: Our Community
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: A community is a place where people live and help one another. In my county there are many places. At the hospital, a doctor helps people who are sick. At the market, people buy and sell food. At the college, older students learn new jobs. An officer keeps us safe on the road. We all live together, and we all help one another.

readings.title: A Poem: “Places I Know”
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
In my village the days are slow,
Past the garden the goats all go.
The market is busy, the court is grand,
So many places across our land!

readings.title: At the Market
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: “On Friday we go to the market. It is busy! We buy bananas and rice. I can see a lot of people. I like the market because it is full of colours.”

readings.title: Community Helpers Song
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Sing along:
“The doctor helps you when you're sick, the officer keeps you safe. The sailor sails across the sea — our helpers make one place!”

readings.title: From Our Village to the County
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Amal lived in a small village near Mount Meru in northern Tanzania. The village was quiet, with tall trees, green farms, and goats walking slowly along the road.
Every Friday, Amal went with her class to learn about the community. One morning, their teacher said, “Today, we will visit the county center.” The whole class clapped. Amal had never been there before.
They climbed into a big school bus and set off. “Look out of the window,” said the teacher. “What can you see?”
“I can see the market,” said Musa. “There are a lot of people!”
“I can see a garden,” said Sophia. “It has flowers and trees.”
Their first stop was the hospital. A kind nurse met them at the door. “This is a public hospital,” she said. “Many people visit here every day.”
Selma raised her hand. “Who works here?” she asked.
The nurse smiled. “The doctor works here. He helps people who are sick.” The class walked through the hall and passed clean rooms with beds, medicine, and smiling workers.
“Exit the hospital quietly,” said the teacher. “People are resting.”
Next, they walked to the court. “What happens here?” asked Sophia.
“This is where problems are solved fairly,” said the teacher. “An officer and a judge work here.” The building was large and painted white. A sign read: “Court — County of Arusha.”
Inside, they sat quietly on the wooden benches. “Can you see the big chair?” asked the teacher. “That is where the judge sits.”
After the court, they walked to the market. Sellers called out beside baskets of bananas, maize, and rice. “This place is busy,” said Amal. “Our village market is much smaller.”
“Some markets are small,” said the teacher, “and others have a lot of things.” They bought sweet mangoes and sat near a small garden to eat.
“Next is the college,” said the teacher.
“What is a college?” asked Musa.
“It is a school for older students,” said the teacher. “There, they learn to be nurses, builders, or officers.”
At the college gate, a friendly student greeted them. “My name is Isaac,” he said. “I study health and safety.”
“Do you want to be a doctor?” asked Sophia.
“Yes,” said Isaac, “because I want to help my village.”
As they walked back to the bus, Amal saw a sign by the road. “What is the border?” she asked.
“It is where one county ends and another begins,” said the teacher. “That is the border between Arusha and Kilimanjaro counties.”
When they returned to school, the teacher said, “Now write about what you saw.” Amal picked up her pencil and wrote:
“Today we went from our village to the county center. We saw a hospital where the doctor works. We visited the court, the college, the market, and a garden. I learned that many people work to help us. I like the officer because he protects us. One day I want to be a sailor and see more places — but I will always come home.”
The teacher smiled at her paper. “This is a good report,” she said. “It has details, and it shows what you learned.”
That evening, Amal shared her writing with her grandmother. “Very nice,” said Grandma. “Your address may say ‘village,’ but your ideas travel far.”
Selma laughed. “Maybe one day I will see the sea, meet a real sailor, or even cross a border,” she said.
Her grandmother nodded gently. “Yes. But never forget your village roots.”

grammar.title: Imperatives (Giving Instructions)
grammar.explanation: An imperative is a special way to use a verb to give a command or an instruction. It tells someone to do something. We need imperatives to give clear directions, like “Exit the court quietly” or “Go to the hospital if you are sick.”
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Start with the base verb (the action word), with no “to”:  Go. / Stop. / Listen.
You do not say “you” — the person already knows you mean them:  “Go to the market.”
You can add details to make it clear:  “Exit the court quietly.”
To be polite, add please:  “Please wash your hands.”
To say *not* to do something, use don't:  “Don't run in the hall.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “You go to the hospital.”  ✗
Fix: Leave out “you” for a command: “Go to the hospital.”  ✓
Mistake: “Quiet in the court.”  ✗   Fix: Don't forget the verb: “Be quiet in the court.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Imperatives are “action words for clear instructions.” If you can picture someone doing the action, you are using the imperative correctly!
grammar.practice: Choose the correct verb to complete each instruction: | ______ your hands before eating.  (Wash / Washing) | ______ the door when you leave.  (Close / Closes) | ______ to the teacher if you have a question.  (Ask / Asking) | ______ your phone during class.  (Use / Don't use)

grammar.title: Simple Present Tense
grammar.explanation: We use the simple present tense to talk about things that happen again and again, or facts that are always true. It helps us describe routines and how our community works, like “The market opens at 7 a.m.”
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use the base verb with: I, you, we, they.
They play football.
We visit the market.
I live in a village.
Add -s (or -es) with: he, she, it.
The doctor works at the hospital.
She visits her grandmother.
The market opens early.
If a verb ends in -y after a consonant, change y to i and add -es: study → studies.
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “The doctor work at the hospital.”  ✗
Fix: he/she/it needs the extra “s”: “The doctor works at the hospital.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: This is your “everyday tense.” If you do it every day, use the simple present. And remember: “s” for he, she, it!
grammar.practice: Write the correct form of the verb in brackets: | My brother ______ (play) football after school. | She ______ (study) maths every day. | The sun ______ (rise) in the east. | They ______ (go) to the market on Fridays. | The nurse ______ (help) sick people.

grammar.title: Present Simple Questions
grammar.explanation: Good learners ask questions! Asking questions helps us find information and understand our community — like “Where is the college?” or “Do you know the address?”
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use do with I, you, we, they:  “Do you visit the market?”
Use does with he, she, it:  “Does she work at the hospital?”
After do/does, use the base verb (no “s”):  “Does he play football?”
You can start with a question word — what, where, when, who, how:  “Where does the doctor work?”
With the verb *to be*, just move is/are to the front:  “Is the hospital open?”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “Does you like the market?”  ✗   Fix: use do for “you”: “Do you like the market?”  ✓
Mistake: “Where he is?”  ✗   Fix: “Where is he?”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: “Do” is the friendly helper for many people (I, you, we, they). “Does” is for the one person who needs extra help (he, she, it).
grammar.practice: Complete each question with do or does and the base verb in brackets: | ______ you ______ (like) the market? | ______ he ______ (play) football? | ______ they ______ (visit) the college? | ______ she ______ (know) the address?

grammar.title: Using “can see” + object
grammar.explanation: We use “can see” to say what we are able to look at and notice around us. It makes our descriptions come alive: “I can see the border,” “She can see the garden from the market.”
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Subject + can + see + object (the thing you see).
I can see the market.
We can see the mountains.
She can see the garden from the window.
To ask: “Can you see the border?”   To say no: “I cannot see the border.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “She cans see the garden.”  ✗   Fix: “can” never adds an “s”: “She can see the garden.”  ✓
Mistake: “I can see.” (with nothing after it)  ✗   Fix: add the object: “I can see the border.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Think of “can see” as your “magic eyes.” Always tell people *what* your magic eyes spot: Subject + can + see + what you see.
grammar.practice: Complete each sentence with can see and finish the idea: | From the bus, I ______ the market. | They ______ the animals at the farm. | We ______ the garden from here. | She ______ the border on the map.

grammar.title: Using “because” for Explanations
grammar.explanation: The little word “because” is very powerful. It explains *why* something happens or why we feel a certain way. For example: “I like the officer because he helps people.”
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Say your idea:  “I like the officer.”
Add because:  “I like the officer because …”
Give the reason:  “… he helps people.”
Full sentence:  “I like the officer because he helps people.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “I like the market. It has fruit.” (two short sentences, no reason word)
Fix: join them with because: “I like the market because it has fruit.”  ✓
Also, “because” cannot stand alone: “Because it rained.” ✗ is not a full sentence. Say “We stayed home because it rained.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Think of “because” as a bridge. It connects your idea to the reason behind it. Every time you want to explain *why*, cross the “because” bridge.
grammar.practice: Finish each sentence with your own reason using “because”: | I like my teacher ______. | The market is my favourite place ______. | I want to be a ______ ______.

grammar.title: Descriptive Adjectives and Opposites + Quantifiers (some, a lot of)
grammar.explanation: Descriptive adjectives are words that describe nouns (people, places, things). Instead of “the village,” we can say “the quiet village.” Opposites help us compare two places clearly.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Pick a noun to describe:  “village.”
Choose an adjective:  “quiet.”
Find its opposite:  the opposite of “quiet” is “busy.”
Show the contrast in one sentence:  “The village is quiet, but the county center is busy.”
Useful opposites for this unit: quiet ↔ busy, small ↔ big, public ↔ private.
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “The village is more quiet than the town.”  ✗
Fix: short words add -er: “The village is quieter than the town.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Picture the two places in your mind: a “quiet village” with soft sounds, and a “busy market” full of people. Seeing the difference helps you remember the words.
grammar.practice: Fill each blank with quiet or busy: | The garden is ______; the market is ______. | My room is ______; the county center is ______. | Lesson 7:  Quantifiers (some, a lot of) | What it means and why it matters | Quantifiers tell us *how much* or *how many*. They help us say whether there is a small amount or a large amount of something in our community. | How to form it | Use some for an amount that is not exact:  “Some counties have many villages.” | Use a lot of for a large amount:  “A lot of people visit the market.” | Both can be used with things you can count (people, villages) and things you cannot count (water). | A common mistake | Mistake: “I do not have some friends.”  ✗ | Fix: in a negative sentence, use any: “I do not have any friends.”  ✓  (Use some in positive sentences and questions.) | Memory tip | Think of some as a little sprinkle, and a lot of as a big pile. | Practice 7 | Fill each blank with some or a lot of: | There are ______ children playing in the garden.  (a large crowd) | I need ______ help with my homework.  (a little) | The market has ______ fruit today.  (a huge amount) | We saw ______ birds near the border.  (just a few)

speaking.title: Speaking 1 — My Community
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Get ready to say:
“In my community there is a ______.”
“At the ______, people ______.”
“I like the ______ because ______.”
Record yourself. Check: Did you name the place clearly? Did you give a reason with “because”? Did you speak loudly enough?
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise my community and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 2 — Giving Directions
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Use imperatives to give simple directions to a place. Say them out loud:
“Go straight to the market.”
“Turn left at the hospital.”
“Exit through the big door.”
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise giving directions and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 3 — Guess the Place
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Play with your AI tutor or your family:
One person thinks of a community place and gives a clue: “A doctor works here.”
The other person guesses: “Is it the hospital?”
Answer: “Yes, it is!” or “No, try again.” Then swap turns.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise guess the place and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 4 — Vocabulary Expert Interview
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Choose six unit words. Ask and answer a clear question about each word using complete sentences.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise vocabulary expert interview and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 5 — Explain and Respond
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Explain one important unit idea, give a reason with because, and respond to two follow-up questions.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise explain and respond and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 6 — One-Minute Unit Talk
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Plan and record a one-minute talk using an opening, three connected details, and a closing sentence.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise one-minute unit talk and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

writing.title: Writing 1 — Describe a Place
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Finish these sentences, then write two more of your own:
The ______ is a place where ______.
It is (quiet / busy).
I like it because ______.
At the ______ I can see ______.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 3 response uses complete connected sentences, precise unit words, and relevant details about describe a place.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used precise Unit vocabulary; organised connected ideas; checked capitals and punctuation; reread and improved my work
writing.support: Plan three ideas, use the word bank, and say each sentence before writing.
writing.extension: Add a stronger opening, two precise details, and a concluding sentence.

writing.title: Writing 2 — Your Own Poem
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write your own version of “Places I Know”. Change the places to ones near your home:
In my ______ the days are slow, / Past the ______ the ______ all go. / The ______ is busy, the ______ is grand, / So many places across our land!
Then draw a picture to go with your poem.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 3 response uses complete connected sentences, precise unit words, and relevant details about your own poem.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used precise Unit vocabulary; organised connected ideas; checked capitals and punctuation; reread and improved my work
writing.support: Plan three ideas, use the word bank, and say each sentence before writing.
writing.extension: Add a stronger opening, two precise details, and a concluding sentence.

writing.title: Writing 3 — A Report
writing.promptAndInstructions: Like Amal in the story, write a short report about a place you visited. Write three or four sentences with details. Begin: “Today I visited the ______.”
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 3 response uses complete connected sentences, precise unit words, and relevant details about a report.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used precise Unit vocabulary; organised connected ideas; checked capitals and punctuation; reread and improved my work
writing.support: Plan three ideas, use the word bank, and say each sentence before writing.
writing.extension: Add a stronger opening, two precise details, and a concluding sentence.

writing.title: Writing 4 — Connected Paragraph
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a six-sentence paragraph about the unit. Begin with a topic sentence and join ideas with and, but or because.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 3 response uses complete connected sentences, precise unit words, and relevant details about connected paragraph.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used precise Unit vocabulary; organised connected ideas; checked capitals and punctuation; reread and improved my work
writing.support: Plan three ideas, use the word bank, and say each sentence before writing.
writing.extension: Add a stronger opening, two precise details, and a concluding sentence.

writing.title: Writing 5 — Information Text
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a heading and five accurate facts. Use precise unit vocabulary and check spelling and punctuation.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 3 response uses complete connected sentences, precise unit words, and relevant details about information text.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used precise Unit vocabulary; organised connected ideas; checked capitals and punctuation; reread and improved my work
writing.support: Plan three ideas, use the word bank, and say each sentence before writing.
writing.extension: Add a stronger opening, two precise details, and a concluding sentence.

writing.title: Writing 6 — Reflect and Improve
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Choose earlier writing, revise three sentences, add two useful details, and explain one improvement you made.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 3 response uses complete connected sentences, precise unit words, and relevant details about reflect and improve.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used precise Unit vocabulary; organised connected ideas; checked capitals and punctuation; reread and improved my work
writing.support: Plan three ideas, use the word bank, and say each sentence before writing.
writing.extension: Add a stronger opening, two precise details, and a concluding sentence.

activities.title: Activities 1 — Who Works Here? (Match)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Match each helper to the place they work. Write the pairs.
doctor  →  ______
officer  →  ______
sailor  →  ______
college student  →  ______
Places: ship · hospital · college · road (or court).
Answers: 1. hospital  2. road/court  3. ship  4. college
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 2 — True or False (Community Facts)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write True or False. If it is false, write the correct answer.
A doctor helps people who are sick.
A market is a place where people learn to swim.
A village is bigger than a county.
An exit is a way to go out of a place.
Answers: 1. True  2. False (a place to buy and sell things)  3. False (a village is smaller)  4. True
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 3 — Fill in the Blanks
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Choose from: hospital · market · border · address · garden.
We buy fruit and vegetables at the ______.
The doctor works at the ______.
Flowers and tomatoes grow in the ______.
I wrote my home ______ on the letter.
The line where two counties meet is the ______.
Answers: 1. market  2. hospital  3. garden  4. address  5. border
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 4 — Quiet or Busy?
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write “quiet” or “busy” for each place. There is no single right answer — explain your choice with “because”. Example: “The village is quiet because there are few people.”
a village
a big market
a garden early in the morning
the county center at noon
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 5 — Vocabulary Sort and Explain
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
activities.instructionsAndItems: Sort twelve unit words into useful categories. Explain the rule for each category in a complete sentence.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 6 — Reading Evidence Hunt
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
activities.instructionsAndItems: Find six important details in the unit texts. Record the detail and explain what it helps the reader understand.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

assignments.title: Places and Community learning portfolio
assignments.instructions: Submit Writing 1 — Describe a Place and a recording of Speaking 1 — My Community. Improve both pieces using the Grade 3 rubrics.
assignments.sourceFile: Derived from unit writing and speaking tasks

quizzes.question: What does 'doctor' mean?
quizzes.options: A person who helps sick people feel better. | A person who works on a boat or ship at sea. | A place where doctors and nurses care for people who are sick or hurt. | A place where people solve problems fairly using the laws.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A person who helps sick people feel better.
quizzes.explanation: The doctor works at the hospital.

quizzes.question: What does 'officer' mean?
quizzes.options: A person who helps keep people safe and makes sure rules are followed. | A place where doctors and nurses care for people who are sick or hurt. | A place where people solve problems fairly using the laws. | A school for older students and adults.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A person who helps keep people safe and makes sure rules are followed.
quizzes.explanation: The officer helped us cross the road.

quizzes.question: What does 'sailor' mean?
quizzes.options: A person who works on a boat or ship at sea. | A place where people buy and sell food and other things. | A place where people solve problems fairly using the laws. | A school for older students and adults.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A person who works on a boat or ship at sea.
quizzes.explanation: The sailor sails across the sea.

quizzes.question: What does 'hospital' mean?
quizzes.options: A place where doctors and nurses care for people who are sick or hurt. | A place where people buy and sell food and other things. | A place where plants, flowers or vegetables grow. | A school for older students and adults.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A place where doctors and nurses care for people who are sick or hurt.
quizzes.explanation: My sister is resting at the hospital.

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Our Community': At the hospital, a _____ helps people who are sick.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: At the hospital, a doctor helps people who are sick.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Our Community': An _____ keeps us safe on the road.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: An officer keeps us safe on the road.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Community Helpers Song': The _____ sails across the sea — our helpers make one place!”
quizzes.explanation: The text says: The sailor sails across the sea — our helpers make one place!”
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'From Our Village to the County': “It has flowers and trees.” Their first stop was the _____.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: “It has flowers and trees.” Their first stop was the hospital.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'A Poem: “Places I Know”': The market is busy, the _____ is grand, So many places across our land!
quizzes.explanation: The text says: The market is busy, the court is grand, So many places across our land!
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Our Community': At the _____, older students learn new jobs.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: At the college, older students learn new jobs.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

teacherNotes.note: Model new language aloud, check meaning before independent work, use mixed-response modes, and give specific feedback on complete sentences, text evidence and vocabulary precision.

“Can you read the poem with me and explain it?”
“Can you play a listening game about community helpers?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you help me practise talking about my community?”
“Can you play ‘Guess the Place’ with me?”
“Can you help me give clear directions to a place?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you check if my sentences are correct?”
“Can you help me add more details to my report?”
“Can you help me make my poem rhyme?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you tell me more jobs that help our community?”
“What does a sailor do all day on a ship?”
“Can you quiz me on the people who help us?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you help me describe the market in my town?”
“What is the difference between a college and my school?”
“Can you quiz me on the places in town?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you help me learn my full address in English?”
“What is the difference between a village and a county?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you quiz me on opposites like quiet and busy?”
“Can you help me use the word ‘public’ in a sentence?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you check my grammar answers?”
“Why do we say ‘the doctor works’ but ‘the doctors work’?”
“Can you give me five more sentences to practise imperatives?”

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: A person who helps sick people feel better. The doctor works at the hospital.
A person who helps keep people safe and makes sure rules are followed. The officer helped us cross the road.
A person who works on a boat or ship at sea. The sailor sails across the sea.
A place where doctors and nurses care for people who are sick or hurt. My sister is resting at the hospital.
A place where people solve problems fairly using the laws. The court is next to the police station.
A school for older students and adults. My big brother goes to college in the city.
A place where people buy and sell food and other things. We bought bananas at the market.
A place where plants, flowers or vegetables grow. There are tomatoes in our school garden.
A door or way to go out of a place; to go out. We used the back exit after the class.
A small group of homes in the countryside, smaller than a town. My grandmother lives in a quiet village.

selfAssessment.statement: I can name places in my community and who works there.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can describe a place using words like quiet and busy.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can give an instruction using an imperative.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can use the simple present (“the doctor works”).
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can ask a question with “do” or “does”.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can say what I can see, and explain a reason with “because”.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself
